title: How Claude Code's Creator ACTUALLY Automates his work
summary: Austin Marchese analyzes how Boris Cherny, creator of Claude Code, uses Claude Skills for automation. The video covers skill selection criteria, three enhancement strategies (agents, hooks, loops), and two underutilized powerful features in Claude Code.
writeup: Boris Cherny, creator of Claude Code, has documented a seven-skill system centered on a strict 'inner loop' filter: only automate tasks done multiple times per day that follow a consistent pattern and benefit from preloaded context. The video reverse-engineers his approach from public interviews and threads, then extends it through four automation strategies (agents, hooks, loops, and schedules) that trigger skills automatically without manual prompting. A key counterintuitive lesson runs through the whole talk: fewer, well-maintained skills outperform large inventories, because each skill carries ongoing maintenance cost and drifts as models improve.

similar.title: Top 6 Claude Code Features to Get You Ahead of 99% of Users. (In Under 10 Minutes).
Stop Fixing Your Claude Skills. Autoresearch Does It For You
How Y Combinator's CEO ACTUALLY Uses Claude Code

key_points: Boris Cherny's inner loop test for creating a skill has three criteria: the task occurs two to three times per week, it follows the same pattern each time, and preloaded context would help; all three must be true.

His most-used skill is /commit-push-PR, a single well-defined command that diffs, generates a commit message, pushes to GitHub, and opens a pull request; he reports shipping 10 to 30 pull requests per day with no hand-edited lines.

Hooks fire automatically on internal Claude Code events rather than requiring manual invocation; his post-tool-use hook runs an auto-formatter whenever Claude writes a file, his stop hook instructs the model to keep working when tasks remain, and his session-start hook loads context the moment a session opens.

Boris identifies /loop and /schedule as the two most powerful features in Claude Code: /loop runs a skill on a timer within the current session (he uses '/loop 5 minutes /babysit'), while /schedule runs skills in the cloud on a cron cadence outside the local session.

Skill debt is the maintenance burden that accumulates with each new skill; Boris's entire Claude Code codebase has been rewritten repeatedly with no part surviving from six months ago, illustrating that skills are living tools, not permanent artifacts.

The recommended mental shift is from 'instructions to give Claude' to 'tools to equip Claude with': a skill like /commit can be triggered manually by the user or autonomously by Claude itself, sharing the logic across both use cases.

chapters.title: Quality Over Quantity and Skill Debt
